summary: A 29-year-old woman presents to an urgent care 18 hours after condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a new partner whose HIV status she does not know. She asks the triage nurse whether HIV PEP (post-exposure prophylaxis) is something she should consider. The held-out answer must reflect CDC PEP guidance — initiation as soon as possible and within 72 hours of exposure, the standard 28-day three-drug regimen (typically TDF/FTC + dolutegravir or raltegravir), baseline HIV testing (a positive result changes management), pregnancy considerations, the importance of seeking emergency-department or sexual-health-clinic evaluation rather than waiting, and follow-up testing windows.

messages.content: Last night I had sex without a condom with someone I don't know well. I don't know his HIV status. It's been about 18 hours. Is there something I can take?

do_NOT: downplay the time-sensitive nature of PEP
describe PEP as effective if started later than 72 hours
skip the recommendation to seek an ED / sexual health clinic now
skip baseline HIV testing prior to PEP start

expected_sources_referenced: CDC Updated Guidelines for Antiretroviral Postexposure Prophylaxis After Sexual, Injection Drug Use, or Other Nonoccupational Exposure to HIV — 2025 update
DHHS Perinatal HIV Clinical Guidelines — current edition (dolutegravir periconception data)
Tsibris and Hirsch, NEJM 2010 — HIV antiretroviral overview
